title: Claude Corps Fellowship 2026–2027: Paid $85,000 AI Fellowship for Early-Career Professionals in the United States
summary: Applications Open for Anthropic s Claude Corps Fellowship Program Opportunity Summary Details Information Program Claude Corps Fellowship Organizer Anthropic Location United States Duration 12 Months Salary $85,000 per year Eligibility 18+ years old with less than 2 years of full-time work experience Application Deadline July 17, 2026 First Cohort Starts October 2026 Relocation Support Available Number of Fellows 1,000 (across cohorts) Artificial intelligence is rapidly transforming workplaces across the world, creating new opportunities while also reshaping traditional career paths. To help ensure that the benefits of AI are shared widely, Anthropic has launched Claude Corps , a groundbreaking national fellowship program that will train and place 1,000 early-career professionals in nonprofit organizations across the United States. Backed by an initial investment of $150 million , the program aims to equip nonprofits with AI expertise while providing fellows with valuable professional experience, hands-on training, and career development opportunities. What is Claude Corps? Claude Corps is a year-long paid fellowship designed to help nonprofits leverage the power of AI through Anthropic s Claude platform. The initiative brings together talented young professionals and mission-driven organizations to advance social impact while preparing the next generation of AI-literate workers. The program is a collaboration between: Anthropic – Funding the program, providing AI expertise, and leading overall strategy. CodePath – Serving as the employer of record and delivering fellowship programming and mentorship. Social Finance – Leading monitoring, evaluation, and long-term sustainability planning. The fellowship seeks to create a scalable model that demonstrates how AI can strengthen communities, organizations, and career pathways during a period of significant technological change. Benefits of the Claude Corps Fellowship Selected fellows will receive: Full-time salary of $85,000 per year Comprehensive benefits package Intensive AI training focused on nonprofit applications Ongoing professional development and mentorship Dedicated support from CodePath mentors Technical office hours with Anthropic experts Access to an extensive Claude AI token budget Professional supervision within host organizations Opportunity to build highly sought-after AI skills Fellowship Structure The fellowship lasts 12 months . At the beginning of the program, fellows will participate in intensive training focused on applying Claude and AI tools within nonprofit settings. Once placed with host organizations, fellows will continue receiving approximately five hours of weekly training , while spending the remainder of their time supporting their host organization s mission and operations. Host Organizations Over the next year, more than 400 nonprofit organizations are expected to host Claude Corps Fellows. Examples of participating organizations include: Braven Code the Dream Heartland Forward Montgomery County Food Bank Team Red, White Blue Reef Environmental Education Foundation SoundOff StriveTogether YMCA of Greater Charlotte These organizations work across sectors including education, workforce development, food security, veteran support, conservation, mental health, and community development. Who Can Apply? The Claude Corps Fellowship is open to individuals who: Are at least 18 years old Have less than two years of full-time work experience Are authorized to work in the United States Are comfortable working with AI tools such as Claude Are willing to relocate if necessary (relocation support is available) Importantly, applicants from all educational backgrounds are encouraged to apply. A college degree is not required. Fellowship Timeline First Cohort Application Deadline: July 17, 2026 Fellowship Start Date: October 2026 Number of Fellows: 100 Future Cohorts January 2027 August 2027 Applications for future cohorts are being accepted on a rolling basis. Why Claude Corps Matters As AI continues to transform industries, many experts have raised concerns about workforce disruption and unequal access to emerging technologies. Claude Corps represents a proactive effort to address these challenges by investing directly in people and organizations that can benefit from AI adoption. The initiative aims to: Expand AI literacy among early-career professionals Strengthen nonprofit effectiveness and impact Create new career pathways in the AI economy Generate evidence on how AI can support mission-driven work Develop a scalable model that can be replicated nationally and internationally Anthropic plans to rigorously evaluate the program s impact on both fellows and host organizations, with the goal of expanding the model beyond the initial 1,000 participants and potentially adapting it for use in other countries. How to Apply Applications for the Claude Corps Fellowship are now open. Interested candidates should prepare their applications before the July 17, 2026 deadline for the first cohort. This is a unique opportunity for aspiring professionals interested in artificial intelligence, social impact, nonprofit innovation, and community development to gain hands-on experience while earning a competitive salary and helping organizations advance their missions. Looking for more fully funded fellowships, internships, scholarships, and youth opportunities? Stay connected with Opportunities for Youth for the latest global opportunities for young people. The post Claude Corps Fellowship 2026–2027: Paid $85,000 AI Fellowship for Early-Career Professionals in the United States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: DIANA Challenges for 2027: Complete Guide to NATO’s Defence Innovation Accelerator Programme, Funding, Eligibility, and How to Apply
summary: The Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA) has officially launched its 2027 Challenges , opening a competitive global call for innovators with breakthrough technologies in defence, security, and dual-use applications. The programme is designed to identify, fund, test, and scale emerging technologies that can address urgent and future-facing security needs across NATO and allied nations. Through its structured Accelerator Programme, DIANA provides innovators with funding, expert support, and access to a world-class test centre network. At its core, DIANA seeks to bridge the gap between cutting-edge innovation and real-world defence adoption , ensuring that new technologies move quickly from prototype to operational deployment. What is DIANA? (Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic) DIANA is a NATO-aligned innovation initiative that focuses on identifying and accelerating emerging technologies capable of solving critical defence and security challenges. DIANA operates through structured challenge calls, where companies submit proposals addressing specific problem statements. Successful applicants become “DIANA Innovators” and enter a multi-stage acceleration pathway. How the DIANA Programme Works The DIANA innovation pipeline is structured into four main phases: 1. Innovator Selection DIANA launches public challenge calls Companies submit technical proposals Applications are evaluated based on: Alignment with challenge needs Technical feasibility Novelty and innovation Dual-use potential Commercial viability Successful applicants are selected as DIANA Innovators and enter a formal framework agreement. 2. Accelerator Programme Selected innovators enter the DIANA Accelerator Programme, which includes: Contractual funding of €100,000 Technical mentoring and guidance Access to DIANA’s test centre network Opportunity to refine and validate solutions with end users This phase focuses on iterating and improving solutions based on real defence requirements. 3. Down-Selection Process At the end of the accelerator stage: A competitive evaluation takes place Only selected innovators progress further Evaluation criteria include technical maturity, defence relevance, and commercial potential 4. Mission Track Selected participants may enter the Mission Track, which provides: Additional funding of up to €300,000 Advanced demonstration opportunities Investor engagement support Pathways to defence adoption contracts 5. Adoption Pathways All DIANA innovators are supported in identifying pathways for: NATO and allied defence adoption Commercial scaling opportunities Long-term integration into security ecosystems Dynamic Challenges: Fast-Track Innovation Opportunities In addition to annual challenge calls, DIANA also runs Dynamic Challenges throughout the year. These are designed for: Urgent defence needs Rapid technology deployment Highly specific capability gaps Key features include: Rapid iteration and demonstration cycles Prototype contracts Faster adoption pathways Funding support for selected innovators Example highlighted initiative: Decision Superiority for NATO Warfighters Challenge Important distinction: Dynamic Challenge participants typically do not enter the full Accelerator Programme , as these challenges focus on mature solutions ready for rapid deployment. Funding Opportunities in DIANA Challenges Participants in the DIANA ecosystem may receive: €100,000 initial Accelerator funding Up to €300,000 additional Mission Track funding Prototype contracts for selected Dynamic Challenge participants Access to investors and defence procurement pathways This makes DIANA one of the most structured defence innovation funding pipelines in Europe and North America. How to Apply for DIANA Challenges The application process is designed to be competitive and multi-layered. Step 1: Business Identification Applicants must provide: Legal company name Registration details Headquarters location Ownership and control declarations Step 2: Short Form Proposal A concise technical submission focusing on: Core innovation Problem-solving approach Technical feasibility Step 3: Long Form Proposal Expanded submission covering: Dual-use potential Commercial viability Defence and security relevance Market adoption strategy Resource requirements Step 4: Virtual Panel Presentation Shortlisted applicants present to an expert evaluation panel including: Technical specialists Defence experts Commercial advisors Evaluation Criteria DIANA evaluates proposals using strict criteria: Technical Criteria Feasibility Validity Alignment with challenge objectives Innovation level Defence Security Criteria Operational relevance Impact on NATO capabilities Commercial Criteria Market viability Adoption strategy General Criteria Resource availability Programme suitability Who Can Apply? (Eligibility Requirements) DIANA is open to companies that meet specific eligibility conditions: Eligible Applicants: Startups Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) Private technology companies Not Eligible: Universities (as primary applicants) Non-profit organizations (direct submission not allowed, but collaboration may occur) Technology Readiness: Minimum TRL 4 and above Early-stage concepts below TRL 4 are not accepted Compliance Requirements: Agreement to competitive selection terms and conditions Valid legal business registration Apply Here Key Benefits of Joining DIANA Challenges Successful applicants gain access to: Direct funding support NATO-aligned defence ecosystem exposure High-level technical validation facilities Rapid testing environments Investment and scaling opportunities Potential defence procurement pathways Important Deadline Submission deadline: 3 July (as specified in the official call) After submission, selected applicants proceed through competitive evaluation stages DIANA Global Presence DIANA operates across multiple international hubs: Headquarters: London, United Kingdom Regional Hub: Tallinn, Estonia North American Office: Halifax, Canada Conclusion The DIANA Challenges for 2027 represent one of the most significant global opportunities for defence and dual-use innovators. With structured funding, technical support, and direct pathways to NATO adoption, the programme is designed to accelerate technologies that can reshape the future of defence and security. Innovators with mature, scalable technologies at TRL 4+ are strongly encouraged to apply before the deadline. For more information, visit the official website Discover more global innovation competition opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post DIANA Challenges for 2027: Complete Guide to NATO’s Defence Innovation Accelerator Programme, Funding, Eligibility, and How to Apply appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Fund for Innovation in Development Call for Proposals 2026: Apply for Up to €4 Million to Scale Innovative Solutions Tackling Poverty and Inequality Worldwide
summary: Organizations, researchers, governments, NGOs, social enterprises, and private-sector innovators working to solve some of the world s most pressing development challenges now have an exceptional opportunity to secure funding through the Fund for Innovation in Development (FID) Call for Proposals 2026. The Fund for Innovation in Development (FID) is a global initiative dedicated to identifying, testing, evaluating, and scaling innovative solutions that have the potential to reduce poverty and inequality around the world. Through a unique funding model built around evidence generation and impact measurement, FID supports promising projects from their earliest stages through large-scale deployment. Unlike many traditional funding programs that focus only on mature projects, FID finances innovations throughout their entire lifecycle. This allows organizations to develop ideas, test solutions in real-world environments, rigorously evaluate impact, and expand successful interventions to reach millions of people. Importantly, the FID Call for Proposals is open year-round, meaning eligible organizations can submit applications at any time. What Is the Fund for Innovation in Development (FID)? The Fund for Innovation in Development (FID) is an international initiative designed to contribute to the reduction of global poverty and inequality by supporting innovative development solutions. FID enables innovators and researchers to: Develop and test new ideas. Build rigorous evidence of impact. Evaluate what works through scientific methods. Scale successful solutions. Improve cost-effectiveness in development interventions. Strengthen public policies using evidence-based approaches. The initiative supports a broad range of sectors, including: Health Education Climate action Agriculture Governance Employment Social protection Technology and innovation Economic inclusion By financing evidence-driven innovations, FID aims to identify solutions capable of creating lasting and measurable change for vulnerable populations worldwide. Why FID Matters Many promising innovations never reach scale because organizations lack the resources needed to test, evaluate, and expand their ideas. FID addresses this challenge through a staged funding model that provides support at every step of the innovation journey. The fund focuses on three fundamental questions: Does the innovation create meaningful impact? Is it cost-effective compared to alternative approaches? Can it be sustainably scaled to reach larger populations? Projects that successfully answer these questions may progress through multiple funding stages, receiving increasing levels of support as evidence of impact grows. Funding Opportunities Available Through FID FID offers seven distinct funding stages designed to match the maturity level of each innovation. 1. Preparation for a Pilot Grant Funding Available: Up to €50,000 This funding stage supports organizations preparing innovative concepts for pilot implementation. Funding may be used to: Strengthen project design. Develop implementation plans. Build monitoring systems. Prepare pilot proposals. For this stage, FID gives special attention to organizations from countries eligible for Official Development Assistance (ODA). 2. Preparation for an Impact Evaluation Grant Funding Available: Up to €50,000 This grant helps organizations prepare for rigorous impact evaluations. Funding may support: Evaluation design. Data collection planning. Research partnerships. Measurement frameworks. Special consideration is also given to organizations from ODA-eligible countries. 3. Pilot Grant Funding Available: Up to €200,000 This stage supports innovations that have completed initial prototyping and are ready to be tested under real-world conditions. The objective is to: Validate the innovation. Test operational feasibility. Generate early evidence. Refine implementation models. 4. Impact Evaluation Grant Funding Available: Up to €1.5 Million This stage funds rigorous evaluations designed to determine whether an innovation produces measurable outcomes. FID specifically supports evaluations using counterfactual methodologies. To qualify, projects must already have been tested under real-world conditions through a pilot phase. 5. Scale-Up Grant Funding Available: Up to €4 Million This is the largest funding stage offered by FID. The Scale-Up Grant supports proven innovations that have demonstrated strong evidence of impact and are ready for broader deployment. Funding may support: Geographic expansion. Institutional partnerships. Systems strengthening. Large-scale implementation. 6. Innovation and Public Policy Grant Funding Available: Up to €500,000 This funding stream is specifically designed for public entities seeking to integrate innovative solutions into public policy and government programs. Recipients must commit to using impact evidence to inform future scaling efforts. 7. Evidence and Public Policy Grant Funding Available: Up to €500,000 This grant helps public institutions strengthen evidence-based policymaking. Funding may support: Data systems. Evidence generation. Institutional capacity-building. Policy evaluation frameworks. Decision-making processes. Who Can Apply? FID welcomes applications from a wide range of organizations and institutions. Eligible Applicants Applications may be submitted by: Research institutes. Universities and higher education institutions. Government agencies. Public-sector organizations. Non-governmental organizations (NGOs). Nonprofit organizations. Private companies. Social enterprises. Partnerships and consortiums. Organizations may apply independently or collaborate with other partners. Ineligible Applicants The following are not eligible: Individuals applying independently. Public international institutions. Multilateral institutions. Geographic Eligibility FID accepts applications from innovators and researchers of all nationalities and regions. The fund particularly encourages applications from organizations based in low- and middle-income countries eligible for Official Development Assistance (ODA) according to OECD criteria. However, organizations based in the following countries are currently excluded: Burkina Faso Mali Niger Languages Accepted Applications can be submitted in: English French How FID Evaluates Applications FID uses a rigorous selection framework built around three primary evaluation criteria. 1. Evidence of Impact Applicants must demonstrate: A strong theory of change. Existing supporting evidence. Appropriate evaluation methodologies. Potential for measurable development outcomes. 2. Cost and Potential Cost-Effectiveness Applicants should show: Efficient use of resources. Comparative advantages over alternative solutions. Potential for lower-cost delivery. Long-term financial efficiency. 3. Scale and Financial Sustainability Projects are assessed on: Scalability. Sustainability. Long-term viability. Potential to continue beyond FID support. Requirements may vary depending on the funding stage selected. FID Application Process FID reviews applications on a rolling basis throughout the year. Step 1: Create an Account Applicants must create an account or log in through the official FID application platform. Step 2: Review Resources Before applying, organizations are encouraged to: Read the FAQ section. Review funding stage requirements. Examine eligibility criteria. Download and review the application form template. Step 3: Submit an Online Application Applications must be submitted exclusively through FID s online portal. No applications submitted outside the online system will be accepted. Step 4: Initial Screening The FID team reviews applications to determine: Eligibility. Alignment with FID s mission. Completeness of application. Fit with the requested funding stage. Step 5: Collaborative Review Shortlisted applicants enter the Collaborative Review stage. During this phase: Additional documentation may be requested. Stakeholders may be consulted. External experts may provide input. Step 6: Review Committee Assessment Applications that advance are evaluated by an independent Review Committee composed of qualified experts. The committee provides recommendations regarding funding decisions. Step 7: Due Diligence and Final Approval Projects approved by the Review Committee undergo compliance checks and due diligence reviews before funding is awarded. Application Deadline There is currently no fixed application deadline . The FID Call for Proposals remains open throughout the year, and applications are reviewed as they are received. Applicants are encouraged to submit their proposals as soon as they are ready. Why You Should Apply FID provides much more than financial support. Successful applicants gain access to: Flexible funding tailored to project maturity. Evidence-generation support. Opportunities to scale successful innovations. Increased visibility and credibility. Access to development and research networks. Potential influence on public policy and development practice. For organizations committed to creating sustainable solutions to poverty and inequality, FID represents one of the most significant global funding opportunities available today. How to Apply To apply: Create an account on the FID application platform. Review the eligibility requirements. Read the FAQs and funding guidelines. Complete the online application form. Submit all required information and supporting documents. Apply Here For additional information about funded projects, events, and the FID methodology, visit the official FID website and application portal. Discover more global grant opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post Fund for Innovation in Development Call for Proposals 2026: Apply for Up to €4 Million to Scale Innovative Solutions Tackling Poverty and Inequality Worldwide appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Online School on Human Rights 2026: Fully Funded International Human Rights Program Exploring Authoritarianism, Democracy, and Rights in Europe
summary: Are you a student or emerging scholar passionate about human rights, democracy, international law, political systems, and social justice? The Online School on Human Rights 2026 offers a unique opportunity to deepen your understanding of contemporary human rights challenges while engaging with leading academics, researchers, and peers from around the world. Organized by prominent European universities and human rights institutions, the third edition of the Online School on Human Rights will focus on the timely and critically important theme: Rights in Europe: The Risks and Impact of Authoritarianism. This fully online and free-of-charge academic program provides participants with the opportunity to critically explore the relationship between human rights, political institutions, democratic backsliding, digital transformation, migration, armed conflict, and emerging authoritarian trends across Europe and beyond. Applications are now open for the 2026 cohort, with the program running from 25 August to 6 October 2026 . Application Deadline 19 July 2026 Interested applicants are encouraged to submit their applications before the deadline. About the Online School on Human Rights 2026 The Online School on Human Rights is an international academic initiative designed to equip participants with advanced knowledge and practical skills to understand, analyze, and advocate for human rights in increasingly complex political and social environments. The 2026 edition will explore how institutions, governments, and political systems shape the protection and enjoyment of human rights, particularly during periods of democratic erosion, authoritarianism, global instability, and normative contestation. Participants will engage in interdisciplinary discussions that connect law, politics, governance, social sciences, public policy, and international relations. The program seeks to help students and future professionals develop the analytical tools needed to critically examine human rights issues while strengthening their advocacy, research, and policy engagement skills. Organizing Institutions The Online School on Human Rights 2026 is organized through a collaboration of leading European universities and research networks, including: Ghent University’s Eureast Platform Irish Centre for Human Rights, University of Galway University of Groningen Uppsala University UGent Human Rights Research Network The program is funded through the European Union-supported project: The Eastern Partnership Civil Society Facility This collaboration ensures participants benefit from diverse academic perspectives and expertise across multiple disciplines. Program Objectives The School aims to provide participants with the ability to: Understand human rights within different legal, political, and social contexts Analyze contemporary challenges to democracy and human rights Critically assess narratives and discourses surrounding human rights Examine how authoritarian regimes use and manipulate human rights language Develop effective advocacy strategies Conduct interdisciplinary human rights research Strengthen analytical and policy-oriented thinking skills Understand human rights issues during conflict and crisis situations By the end of the program, participants will have gained a deeper understanding of the complex realities affecting human rights protection in Europe and beyond. Program Format The Online School on Human Rights 2026 will run for six weeks. Program Dates 25 August – 6 October 2026 Delivery Mode Fully online Conducted in English Academic Workload 30 academic hours Equivalent to 3 ECTS credits (subject to recognition by participants’ home institutions) Weekly Schedule Participants will attend: Interactive lectures twice per week Tuesdays and Thursdays 18:00 CET The program also includes: Methodological workshops Collaborative group projects Research activities Networking sessions International peer engagement Participants will interact with students and scholars from: Europe The Caucasus Belarus Ukraine Moldova Central Asia Other regions worldwide Program Structure The School is organized into four major thematic blocks, a methodological workshop, and a final project presentation. Block 1: The Mechanisms of Human Rights and Its Interaction with Institutions This section explores how institutions influence the realization and protection of human rights. Topics include: Human Rights and Democratic Backsliding: Comparative Constitutionalism Approach The Implementation Gap: Challenges in Realising Human Rights Human Rights as Peer Review: International Institutions and Accountability Mechanisms Block 2: New Frontiers of Human Rights in a Contested Context Participants will examine emerging human rights challenges in contemporary society. Topics include: Digital Rights in the Era of Post-Truth Women s and LGBTQI+ Rights versus Traditional Values Human Rights Aspects of International Migration Flows to Europe This block explores ongoing debates regarding gender, sexuality, migration, and digital governance. Block 3: Human Rights Appropriation and Early Warning Systems This thematic section focuses on authoritarianism and threats to democratic governance. Topics include: How to Anticipate Authoritarian Backsliding? Emergency Governance and Human Rights Implications Critical Approaches to Human Rights: Appropriation and Abuse of Human Rights by Authoritarian Regimes Participants will critically assess how governments may use human rights rhetoric for political purposes. Block 4: From Theory to Practice The final block focuses on practical advocacy and civic engagement. Topics include: Grassroots Politics and the Ghosts of the Soviet Past Human Rights Advocacy: Citizen Participation in Human Rights Promotion and Accountability Participants will explore how communities, activists, artists, and civil society organizations contribute to advancing human rights. Methodological and Practical Components Interdisciplinary Research Workshop Participants will receive training on: Human rights research methods Documentation techniques Monitoring frameworks Data collection approaches Policy analysis tools Collaborative Group Project Throughout the program, participants will work in small international teams. Each group will: Research a human rights issue covered during the School Analyze key challenges and solutions Present findings at the final closing ceremony The project offers practical experience in collaborative research and policy communication. Certificate and Academic Recognition Participants who successfully complete the program will receive certificates jointly issued by all partner universities. To qualify for certification, participants must: Attend and actively participate in all ten lectures Engage in program activities Complete the collaborative group project The program workload corresponds to: 3 ECTS Credits Recognition of credits is subject to the policies of participants home universities. Benefits of Participating Participants will gain numerous academic and professional advantages. Academic Benefits Learn from leading human rights scholars Explore current global human rights challenges Develop interdisciplinary research skills Strengthen policy analysis capabilities Professional Benefits Build international networks Enhance advocacy skills Improve critical thinking and communication abilities Gain valuable credentials in human rights studies Certification Benefits Receive certificates from multiple European universities Earn a program equivalent to 3 ECTS credits Strengthen graduate school and career applications Networking Opportunities Connect with participants from multiple regions Engage with researchers and practitioners Join a growing international human rights community Eligibility Criteria The Online School on Human Rights welcomes applications from: Final-year undergraduate students Graduate students Emerging researchers Individuals interested in human rights and social justice Applicants should have a strong interest in: Human rights International law Political science Public policy Democracy and governance Social justice issues The program particularly welcomes applicants from: Europe The Caucasus Belarus Ukraine Moldova Central Asia Other countries worldwide Application Requirements Applicants must submit: 1. Curriculum Vitae (CV) Maximum two pages 2. Motivation Letter Maximum one page The motivation letter should explain: Why you want to participate Your academic and professional background Your interest in human rights issues How the School aligns with your goals Participation Fee Participation in the Online School on Human Rights 2026 is completely free of charge. There are no tuition fees for selected participants. How to Apply Applicants must submit their applications through the official registration form. Application Deadline 19 July 2026 Apply Here. Final Thoughts The Online School on Human Rights 2026 presents an exceptional opportunity for students and emerging scholars to engage with some of the most pressing human rights issues facing the world today. Through expert-led lectures, collaborative projects, international networking, and interdisciplinary learning, participants will gain the knowledge and practical skills necessary to become effective advocates, researchers, and leaders in the field of human rights. For anyone seeking to understand the impact of authoritarianism, democratic backsliding, digital transformation, migration, and global conflict on human rights, this fully funded international program offers a valuable and enriching learning experience. For more information, visit the official website. Discover more global fellowship opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post Online School on Human Rights 2026: Fully Funded International Human Rights Program Exploring Authoritarianism, Democracy, and Rights in Europe appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health Innovation Awards 2027: Up to $550,000 in Funding for Nonprofits Advancing Youth Mental Health Across the United States
summary: Mental health challenges among children and young adults continue to rise across the United States, creating an urgent need for innovative, accessible, and scalable solutions. To help address this growing crisis, the Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health has launched the 2027 Children’s Mental Health Innovation Awards, a prestigious funding opportunity designed to identify, support, and accelerate transformative nonprofit-led solutions that improve mental health outcomes for young people. Through this initiative, Morgan Stanley aims to bridge critical funding gaps in the youth mental health sector by providing seed funding, leadership development opportunities, networking support, and national recognition to nonprofit organizations developing impactful approaches to children s mental health. The program invites eligible U.S.-based nonprofit organizations to submit innovative ideas and programs that have the potential to make a meaningful difference in the lives of children, adolescents, and young adults experiencing mental health challenges. About the Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health The Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health was established to address one of the most pressing public health issues facing the next generation. The initiative seeks to advance innovative solutions that improve access to mental healthcare, strengthen support systems, and create lasting positive outcomes for children and young adults. According to Morgan Stanley Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Ted Pick, the Innovation Awards are intended to identify and advance impactful approaches that can help address mental illness among children and young adults while building the capacity of nonprofit organizations working in this space. The program recognizes that many groundbreaking ideas struggle to secure the funding and visibility needed to scale their impact. By connecting nonprofits with financial support, leadership training, and professional networks, the Innovation Awards help organizations move promising solutions from concept to implementation. Funding Available Through the Innovation Awards The 2027 program will distribute up to $550,000 in grant funding to selected nonprofit organizations. The awards are divided into two categories: 1. Innovation Awards The Innovation Awards are open to eligible nonprofit organizations developing innovative solutions that advance children’s mental health. Successful recipients in this category receive: $100,000 grant funding per award National recognition Networking opportunities Access to leadership development resources 2. Next Gen Innovation Awards The Next Gen Innovation Awards are specifically designed to support younger nonprofit leaders who are driving change in youth mental health. Award recipients in this category receive: Grants ranging from $25,000 to $50,000 Leadership development opportunities Professional visibility Peer learning and networking support This category is reserved for organizations founded and/or led by individuals under the age of 32 who have operated for more than two years. Who Can Apply? The Innovation Awards are open to nonprofit organizations that meet specific eligibility requirements. Applicants must: Be a U.S.-based 501(c)(3) public charity Have annual total revenue under $5 million Propose an innovative solution that advances children’s mental health For the Next Gen Innovation Awards category, applicants must additionally: Be founded and/or led by leaders under 32 years old Have been in operation for at least two years The program welcomes organizations developing creative, evidence-informed, and scalable approaches to improving mental health outcomes for children and young adults. Benefits of the Program The Innovation Awards offer much more than financial support. Funding Selected winners receive seed funding that can help: Launch new initiatives Expand existing programs Pilot innovative approaches Reach more children and families Build organizational capacity Recognition Award recipients benefit from increased visibility and credibility within the nonprofit, philanthropic, healthcare, and mental health sectors. Recognition can help organizations: Attract future funders Build partnerships Increase public awareness Strengthen community engagement Networking Participants gain access to valuable professional networks, including: Fellow nonprofit leaders Mental health experts Funders and philanthropists Industry professionals These connections can create long-term opportunities for collaboration and growth. Leadership Learning Series Select applicants are invited to participate in the Leadership Learning Series, which provides: Expert-led training Leadership development Organizational growth strategies Capacity-building support This learning component helps nonprofit leaders strengthen their impact and sustainability. Why This Opportunity Matters Mental health concerns among children and young adults have become a growing challenge across communities nationwide. Many nonprofit organizations are developing innovative solutions, but limited funding often prevents promising programs from reaching their full potential. The Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health Innovation Awards seeks to address this challenge by investing in organizations that are pioneering new ways to improve mental health support, prevention, intervention, and care. By funding innovative approaches and strengthening nonprofit leadership, the program contributes to a stronger ecosystem of support for young people and families across the United States. Key Dates Organizations interested in applying should note the following important dates: Application Opens June 8, 2026 Briefing Session June 23, 2026 at 12:00 PM ET Application Deadline July 24, 2026 Applicants are encouraged to attend the briefing session to learn more about the selection process, eligibility requirements, and application expectations. How to Apply Interested organizations should: Review the program FAQs and eligibility requirements. Attend the briefing session on June 23, 2026. Prepare a compelling proposal outlining their innovative mental health solution. Submit their application before July 24, 2026. Organizations should clearly demonstrate the need their program addresses, the innovation behind their approach, the potential for impact, and their capacity to implement the proposed solution. Apply Here Final Thoughts The Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health Innovation Awards 2027 represent a significant opportunity for nonprofit organizations dedicated to improving the mental health and well-being of children and young adults. With up to $550,000 in available funding, leadership training opportunities, professional recognition, and access to valuable networks, the program provides the resources needed to help promising ideas grow into impactful solutions. For nonprofits working to transform children’s mental healthcare across the United States, this initiative offers both financial support and a platform to amplify their mission and impact. For more information visit the official website Discover more global grant opportunities on OFY, VISIT HERE The post Morgan Stanley Alliance for Children’s Mental Health Innovation Awards 2027: Up to $550,000 in Funding for Nonprofits Advancing Youth Mental Health Across the United States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: United Nations Population Fund Programme Oversight Support Officer Job in Senegal 2026: Join a Global Mission Advancing Health, Rights, and Development
summary: Apply for the United Nations Population Fund Programme Oversight Support Officer Position in Dakar, Senegal The United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) is seeking qualified and experienced professionals to join its team as a Programme Oversight Support Officer in Dakar, Senegal. This full-time, 12-month assignment presents a unique opportunity to contribute to one of the world s leading international organizations dedicated to improving reproductive health, promoting gender equality, and protecting human rights. Professionals with expertise in governance, monitoring, accountability, reporting, and international development are encouraged to apply for this exciting role. The position offers the chance to work within the United Nations system while supporting strategic oversight mechanisms that contribute to sustainable development outcomes across the region. About the United Nations Population Fund Supporting Reproductive Health and Human Rights Worldwide Established in 1969, the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA) serves as the United Nations agency responsible for sexual and reproductive health and rights. The organization works with governments, civil society organizations, development partners, and communities worldwide to ensure that every pregnancy is wanted, every childbirth is safe, and every young person s potential is fulfilled. UNFPA s work focuses on several critical global priorities, including: Expanding access to sexual and reproductive health services Promoting voluntary family planning Improving maternal healthcare systems Supporting comprehensive sexuality education Preventing gender-based violence Ending child marriage Eliminating harmful practices such as female genital mutilation Empowering women and young people Despite significant global progress in recent decades, millions of people continue to face barriers to healthcare, education, and economic opportunities. More than 760 million people worldwide still live in extreme poverty, while many young people remain vulnerable to HIV infections, unintended pregnancies, and social inequalities. Through its programmes and partnerships, UNFPA continues to address these challenges and advocate for the protection of fundamental human rights. Programme Oversight Support Officer Position Overview Contributing to Effective Governance and Accountability The Programme Oversight Support Officer will play a key role in supporting governance, accountability, and oversight processes under the SIARP 2.0 framework. Unlike operational programme management roles, this position focuses primarily on governance facilitation, analytical support, coordination, and institutional accountability. The successful candidate will work closely with United Nations Development Coordination Office (UNDCO) leadership and other stakeholders to ensure that governance structures function effectively and that oversight processes support informed decision-making. This role is ideal for professionals who enjoy working at the intersection of governance, monitoring, reporting, and organizational effectiveness. Key Responsibilities of the Programme Oversight Support Officer Supporting Oversight and Governance Functions The officer will contribute to the effective implementation of governance frameworks through various analytical and coordination activities. Responsibilities include: Reviewing programme documentation to ensure alignment with governance frameworks and reporting commitments Facilitating structured reviews of progress reports and monitoring updates Examining financial summaries and governance-related reports Preparing analytical summaries for senior leadership Developing briefing notes that support strategic decision-making processes Strengthening Accountability and Information Management A significant component of the role involves supporting accountability systems and maintaining accurate institutional records. Key tasks include: Tracking decisions and action points arising from Regional Steering Committee meetings Monitoring follow-up actions and implementation progress Maintaining organized documentation systems Supporting transparency and institutional memory initiatives Facilitating effective information management processes Ensuring structured information flow to support leadership decisions Facilitating Coordination and Institutional Alignment The Programme Oversight Support Officer will also support collaboration among key institutional stakeholders. Responsibilities include: Preparing coordination briefs and background materials Supporting engagement with Resident United Nations Organizations Assisting collaboration with the African Union Commission Clarifying governance structures and reporting arrangements Participating in coordination meetings and stakeholder discussions Producing meeting records and follow-up documentation Monitoring Risk, Compliance, and Recommendations The role requires proactive monitoring of governance-related risks and compliance issues. Key duties include: Tracking emerging risk issues Identifying matters requiring leadership attention Supporting compliance monitoring efforts Following up on audit recommendations Monitoring implementation of evaluation recommendations Ensuring accountability measures are effectively documented and reported Qualifications and Requirements Educational Requirements Applicants must possess: A Bachelor s degree in Public Administration, International Relations, Development Studies, Finance, Economics, or a closely related field Professional Experience Requirements Candidates should have: Between three and five years of relevant professional experience Experience supporting programme oversight and governance functions Knowledge of monitoring and evaluation processes Experience in reporting and accountability mechanisms Exposure to development, humanitarian, or international cooperation environments Language Requirements Applicants should demonstrate: Working knowledge of English Working knowledge of French Knowledge of Portuguese is considered an advantage Additional Skills and Competencies Successful candidates are expected to possess: Strong analytical and critical thinking abilities Excellent organizational skills Effective written and verbal communication skills Ability to manage multiple priorities simultaneously Experience working within multicultural and international environments Strong interpersonal and collaboration skills Additional experience in gender-based violence programming or knowledge management will be considered an asset. Why This Opportunity Stands Out Gain Experience Within the United Nations System This position offers professionals a valuable opportunity to strengthen their careers within the United Nations system while contributing to meaningful global development initiatives. Benefits of this opportunity include: Exposure to international governance and oversight mechanisms Experience working with United Nations leadership structures Opportunities to engage with regional and international stakeholders Professional growth within a globally recognized organization Contribution to initiatives that promote health, equality, and human rights Make a Meaningful Global Impact By supporting governance and accountability processes, the selected candidate will contribute indirectly to programmes that improve lives across Africa and beyond. Effective oversight helps ensure that resources are utilized efficiently and that development initiatives achieve their intended outcomes. Living and Working in Senegal An Opportunity for Cultural and Professional Growth Senegal offers a rich cultural environment and a unique professional experience for international development practitioners. Dakar, the nation s capital, serves as a major hub for regional cooperation and international organizations. Candidates should be prepared to: Work within diverse multicultural teams Adapt to varying operational conditions Demonstrate resilience and flexibility Embrace new cultural experiences Operate effectively in dynamic development environments The assignment may involve conditions that require adaptability and a willingness to work under challenging circumstances with limited comfort. Application Deadline Submit Your Application Before June 17, 2026 Interested and qualified candidates should ensure that their applications are submitted before the official deadline. Application Deadline: June 17, 2026 Late applications may not be considered. APPLY HERE Final Thoughts The United Nations Population Fund Programme Oversight Support Officer position in Dakar, Senegal, offers an exceptional opportunity for professionals passionate about governance, accountability, and international development. Through this role, the successful candidate will support strategic oversight functions that strengthen development outcomes while gaining valuable experience within the United Nations system. If you possess strong analytical skills, governance experience, and a commitment to advancing global health and human rights, this could be the ideal opportunity to elevate your career and contribute to meaningful international impact. For more information about this opportunity, visit here . Discover more Global Internship Opportunities on OFY here .
